title: Getting started: Imaging (Micro-Manager) IX85-LTTL
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"bkfsn","text":"The IX85-LTTL microscopes are fully motorised widefield fluorescence microscopes optimised for live cell imaging applications. ","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
steps: The FIND_SAMPLE button runs a script that will attempt to detect the bottom of the coverslip with the ZDC and move to a near focus position.
Mount your sample
Move the XY stage joystick so that your sample is over the objective
Press FIND_SAMPLE button
This script will change to the 10x objective, perform a find-focus-with-offset routine using the ZDC and then return to the previous objective
Manually adjust focus
Select a preset channel setting (1.Imaging_channel group)
Adjust XY stage position and focus as required
Press MEASURE_OFFSET button
The ZDC will measure the offset value between the coverslip and current focal plane
Exposure time
LED intensity

Kinetix sCMOS camera modes Mode Bit depth Frame rate (fps) Full well capacity Read noise Dark current 1 Dynamic Range 16-bit 83 fps 15000 e‾ 1.6 e‾ 1.27 e‾/p/sec 2 Sensitivity 12-bit 88 fps 1000 e‾ 1.2 e‾ 1.03 e‾/p/sec 3 Speed 8-bit 498 fps 200 e‾ 2.0 e‾ 3 e‾/p/sec 4 Sub-Electron 16-bit 5 fps 1000 e‾ 0.7 e‾ 0.477 e‾/p/sec The different camera modes allow you to choose an optimal setting for your experiment and analysis
Increasing the exposure time increases the amount of time that the sample is exposed to light and how long the camera is collecting light.
Longer exposure times mean higher signal.
Longer exposure times increases the light dosage (i.e. higher phototoxicity) and also slows down acquisition speed.
Increasing LED intensity means the sample is exposed to more light.
Higher intensity will increase brightness of the image but also increase light dosage, leading to increased phototoxicity.
For most experiments, the default Dynamic Range camera mode will be most suitable.
Sensitivity mode has lower read noise and dark current, suitable for lower signals.
Speed allows very fast imaging. Note: values are for the full frame, higher frame rates can be achieved with all modes with cropped regions.
Sub-Electron mode is suitable when single molecule sensitivity is required.
Resolution and sampling rate

Optical resolution is determined by the NA of the objective, but sampling rate (pixel size) is determined by the camera and the magnification of the whole system. As well as objective magnification, the IX85 microscope has an optivar with additional magnifications (1x, 1.6x and 2x). These allow optimal sampling rates for each objective. Objective magnification Objective NA Optivar (optimal) Pixel size 1 4x 0.16 2x 813nm 2 10x 0.4 2x 325nm 3 20x 0.45 1.6x 203nm 4 20x 0.8 2x 163nm 5 40x 0.6 1x 163nm 6 40x 0.95 1.6x 102nm Optivar magnification to achieve optimal pixel size (optional)
